Metadata.Case_Number: Civil Appeal No 19 of 2024; Suit No 953 of 2020
Metadata.Coram: Tay Yong Kwang JCA, Woo Bih Li JAD and Kannan Ramesh JAD
Metadata.Tribunal_Court: Appellate Division of the High Court of Singapore

Party_Details.Name: Inter-Pacific Petroleum Pte Ltd (in liquidation)

Facts.Text: Inter-Pacific Petroleum Pte Ltd was incorporated in Singapore on 28 June 2011.
Inter-Pacific Petroleum Pte Ltd had two lines of business, namely cargo trading and bunker trading.
The cargo trading business of Inter-Pacific Petroleum Pte Ltd involved back-to-back purchase and sale of fuel oil with delivery made directly by suppliers to customers.
The bunker trading business of Inter-Pacific Petroleum Pte Ltd involved purchase and delivery of bunker fuel ex-wharf in bulk, followed by breaking bulk and selling parcels to customers.
Inter-Pacific Petroleum Pte Ltd's bunker trading operations depended on the company maintaining a bunker supplier licence and a bunker craft operator licence.
Inter-Pacific Petroleum Pte Ltd obtained financing facilities from Societe Generale and Malayan Banking Berhad for its trading businesses.
An audit confirmation request addressed to Mercuria Energy Trading Pte Ltd was signed by Dr Goh on 7 February 2018 to confirm receivables allegedly due to IPP.
The audited financial statements for the year ended 31 December 2017 recorded that no receivables were impaired and trade debtors were of high credit ratings.
MPA discovered tampering with the mass flow meter of a bunker tanker chartered by IPP during an enforcement check on 13 June 2019.
On 27 June 2019, MPA temporarily suspended IPP's Bunker Craft Operator Licence.
Between 21 June 2019 and 2 August 2019, IPP made drawdowns on its facilities in the sum of US$146,047,099.60 for cargo trades.
On 25 March 2021, IPP was placed in compulsory liquidation.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether a company director is liable for corporate losses resulting from massive drawdowns on bank facilities for sham trades when the director was unaware of that specific line of business.
Party_Details.Rule: A director owes a duty to exercise reasonable skill, care, and diligence, which includes an obligation to remain informed about all significant business operations. However, liability for breach of this duty requires establishes a causal link between the director's ignorance and the specific loss suffered. This requires a counterfactual analysis showing that the loss would have been avoided had the director fulfilled their duty.
Party_Details.Application: Inter-Pacific Petroleum Pte Ltd suffered catastrophic losses from cargo trading drawdowns exceeding US$146 million, which were used to fund sham transactions. While the director, Goh Jin Hian, was actively managing the bunker business and responding to regulatory suspensions, he was completely unaware of the cargo trading division. The financial facilities with Malayan Banking Berhad were dedicated to these cargo trades. Despite signing audit confirmations and being involved in board-level strategy, the director did not appreciate the existence or scope of the cargo trading operations. These operations were subsequently found to involve deep-seated fraud that had escaped detection by the company's own auditors.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: A director's lack of awareness regarding a core business line that accounts for the majority of a company's debt constitutes a breach of the objective duty of care. However, liability for corporate loss is not an automatic consequence of such a breach. Establishing causation requires a detailed hypothetical scenario demonstrating that a reasonably diligent director would have detected the fraud and intervened to stop the drawdowns. Where the underlying transactions involve sham trades supported by sophisticated irregularities that auditors failed to uncover, the mere assumption of awareness is insufficient to prove the trades would have been stopped. In the absence of a specific counterfactual showing that the director's awareness would have led to the discovery and prevention of the sham transactions, the causal link between the breach of duty and the financial loss remains unproven.

Metadata.Case_Number: Civil Appeal No 19 of 2024; Suit No 953 of 2020
Metadata.Coram: Tay Yong Kwang JCA, Woo Bih Li JAD and Kannan Ramesh JAD
Metadata.Tribunal_Court: Appellate Division of the High Court of Singapore

Party_Details.Name: Goh Jin Hian

Facts.Text: Goh Jin Hian was a director of Inter-Pacific Petroleum Pte Ltd from 28 June 2011.
From around July 2015, Goh Jin Hian transitioned to a role he described as non-executive director while remaining on the board.
Goh Jin Hian signed an audit confirmation request for Mercuria Energy Trading Pte Ltd receivables for the 2017 financial year.
On 13 November 2018, Goh Jin Hian discussed bunker profit and chartering income with the CFO in relation to a potential acquisition, without reference to cargo trading.
Goh Jin Hian emailed MPA appealing for reconsideration of the bunker licence suspension, describing it as having a severe impact on IPP.
On 17 July 2019, Goh Jin Hian signed a confirmation of indebtedness to Malayan Banking Berhad confirming amounts outstanding under the Maybank Facility.
On 22 August 2019, after meeting with banks, Goh Jin Hian reported to the police a debt exposure of US$150 million and the possibility of fraud in some transactions.
In a February 2020 interview, Goh Jin Hian stated his assumption that the company was only in the bunker industry and that the bank lines he signed were for bunker-related activities.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether a director's breach of the duty of care by failing to monitor a core business line makes them liable for losses resulting from trades in that division which the director was unaware of and did not authorize.
Party_Details.Rule: A director’s duty to exercise reasonable diligence requires them to acquire and maintain enough knowledge of the business to monitor management. However, the director is not liable for all corporate losses; there must be proof that the director's specific breach of duty was the proximate cause of the loss. The duty to consider creditors' interests is only triggered when a director exercises discretion to cause the company to enter into transactions while in financial distress.
Party_Details.Application: Goh Jin Hian served as a director and was identified as the 'strategic leader' of the company, yet his awareness was limited entirely to the bunker trading line. He signed audit and bank debt confirmations for tens of millions of dollars without understanding that these funds were specifically financing a separate cargo trading division. He did not authorize the specific drawdowns of US$146 million and was unaware of the transactions at the time they occurred. The company’s exposure to these debts was only realized after discussions with banks post-resignation, where it was discovered that the underlying cargo trades were shams.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: While a sustained lack of awareness regarding a significant business operation constitutes a breach of the statutory duty of care, liability requires the establishment of a causal connection to the loss. This necessitates a counterfactual analysis to prove that a diligent director would have uncovered the sham transactions and intervened. If the fraud is sufficiently deep-seated to avoid detection by standard auditing procedures and the director did not exercise any active discretion in the specific transactions causing the loss, the creditor-focused duty is not breached. In the absence of evidence that the director's awareness would have led to the detection of sham trades that deceived professional auditors, the breach of the duty of care does not establish legal responsibility for the resulting financial loss. Consequently, the director is not liable for damages where the causal link between their ignorance and the specific drawdown remains unproven.
